patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 67-year-old retired auto mechanic. You speak vaguely and are a bit evasive; give short answers unless prompted. Start by saying your hands shake and you’re moving slower. Volunteer: tremor is worse at rest (watching TV), morning stiffness/slow start, trouble with buttons/shoelaces, slower shuffling walk, feeling more anxious/on edge. If asked directly, add: freezing when starting/turning, near-falls/one fall, smaller messy handwriting, softer voice, reduced arm swing, constipation, reduced smell, drooling at rest, lightheaded standing, acting out dreams. Keep hidden unless the clinician asks specifically about swallowing, bladder urgency/incontinence, memory/routines, flat mood, dry eyes/blinking less, stiff facial expression, foot cramps/twisting. Resist discussing bedwetting, sexual issues, mild hallucinations, and night overeating; deny at first (“not really”) then admit if asked gently. Mention misleading issues if broad ROS: constant thirst/peeing, migraine-like headaches with nausea/light sensitivity and occasional arm weakness, lifelong slow learning, weight gain. Express cost misconceptions: opicapone is a brand-name upsell; stretching doses saves money; assume insurance won’t help; fear side effects mean costly tests. If the clinician shows empathy, soften and share more but still worry about costs.

medications: Carbidopa/levodopa 25/100 mg 1.5 tablets by mouth five times daily
Opicapone 50 mg by mouth nightly
Pramipexole 0.5 mg by mouth three times daily

Cognitive impairment and intellectual disability limiting history reliability and masking depression/apathy
Drug-induced parkinsonism risk from dopamine-blocking agents (e.g., antipsychotics/antiemetics); metoclopramide intolerance noted
Essential tremor overlap (action tremor) potentially obscuring assessment of resting tremor severity
REM sleep behavior disorder and fragmented sleep contributing to daytime somnolence and attention deficits

differentials: developmental delay, intellectual disability, obesity, and dysmorphic features

No red flags suggesting atypical parkinsonism predominates (e.g., early falls, early severe autonomic failure, supranuclear gaze palsy, poor levodopa response)

must_include: Parkinson disease is a chronic neurodegenerative disorder causing bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor and gait changes

Common adverse effects: dyskinesia, nausea, constipation/diarrhea, dizziness/orthostasis, hallucinations/psychosis, insomnia

Discuss fall risk and orthostatic symptoms; advise slow position changes and hydration as appropriate
Clarify when to seek urgent care: severe confusion, hallucinations with unsafe behavior, syncope, severe uncontrolled dyskinesia
Exercise is disease-modifying for function and reduces disability; provide a realistic plan

Medication timing adherence; keep a symptom diary of ON/OFF periods to guide adjustments

If appropriate after safety checks: initiate opicapone as add-on for wearing-off and provide clear dosing/timing instructions and monitoring plan; avoid contraindicated dopamine blockers (explicitly metoclopramide).

Arrange follow-up to assess OFF time, dyskinesia, hallucinations, orthostasis, adherence, and need for levodopa dose adjustment; provide earlier review if adverse effects occur.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Before any prescribing, the agent performs and documents all three critical safety checks: allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check.

The agent explicitly states that metoclopramide is contraindicated/should be avoided because it worsens parkinsonism, and offers safer alternatives conceptually (without prescribing dopamine-blockers).
The agent explains Parkinson disease as a chronic neurodegenerative disorder causing bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor, and gait changes, in patient-friendly language.
The agent discusses motor fluctuations and wearing-off, noting that fluctuations can occur over the day and are common with long-term levodopa therapy.

The agent mentions differential features/red flags that would prompt additional workup (e.g., prominent polyuria/polydipsia for diabetes insipidus; progressive cognitive/developmental issues; atypical parkinsonism red flags).
When describing opicapone, the agent correctly identifies it as a once-daily COMT inhibitor used to prolong levodopa effect and reduce OFF time.
The agent counsels on common adverse effects of opicapone: dyskinesia, nausea, constipation/diarrhea, dizziness/orthostasis, hallucinations/psychosis, and insomnia.
The agent instructs the patient to contact the clinician promptly if dyskinesia or hallucinations occur after starting opicapone and notes that levodopa dose adjustment may be needed.
The agent reviews and incorporates allergy history into medication choices, specifically penicillin (hives) and metoclopramide reaction (severe worsening of parkinsonism).
The agent assesses fall risk and orthostatic symptoms and provides advice on slow position changes and hydration as appropriate.
The agent provides clear urgent-care/ED return precautions: severe confusion, hallucinations with unsafe behavior, syncope, or severe uncontrolled dyskinesia.
The agent provides lifestyle counseling that exercise improves function and reduces disability, offers a realistic exercise plan, and recommends PT/OT and home fall-prevention strategies.
The agent emphasizes medication timing adherence and recommends keeping a symptom diary of ON/OFF periods to guide future adjustments.

The agent avoids unsafe recommendations (e.g., dopamine antagonists for nausea, abrupt cessation of dopaminergic therapy) and uses shared decision-making with teach-back or confirmation of understanding.

minimal_paths.description: Identify wearing-off in Parkinson disease on levodopa, complete required safety checks, initiate opicapone add-on, and provide comprehensive counseling and follow-up.
must_collect: Description of OFF episodes (timing relative to doses, duration, frequency)
Allergy history including penicillin reaction and metoclopramide reaction
Red-flag symptoms for atypical parkinsonism or alternative diagnoses
steps: ASK: characterize motor symptoms, wearing-off pattern, function, and current meds
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm allergies and reactions (penicillin hives; metoclopramide worsens parkinsonism)
CHECK_INTERACTION: screen for interacting/contraindicated meds and dopamine blockers
ASK: contraindication-focused screen (psychosis, severe dyskinesia, orthostasis/syncope, hepatic disease)
DIAGNOSE: Parkinson disease with motor fluctuations (wearing-off); note absence/presence of red flags
PRESCRIBE: opicapone add-on with dosing/timing and monitoring; explicitly avoid metoclopramide
EDUCATE: diagnosis, treatment, adverse effects/when to call, fall precautions, exercise/PT/OT, symptom diary
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: reassess in a defined interval (e.g., 2–6 weeks) or sooner if adverse effects

minimal_paths.description: Recognize wearing-off but identify high-risk features (active hallucinations/psychosis or severe dyskinesia/unsafe falls) and defer opicapone; prioritize safety, medication review, and urgent/expedited follow-up.
must_collect: Active hallucinations/psychosis severity and safety risk
Current levodopa dosing and recent changes
Current medication list (dopamine blockers, sedatives, anticholinergics)
steps: ASK: assess OFF pattern plus neuropsychiatric symptoms, dyskinesia, falls
DIAGNOSE: PD with fluctuations plus complication (psychosis/dyskinesia/fall risk)
EDUCATE: explain diagnosis and why opicapone may worsen dyskinesia/hallucinations; avoid metoclopramide; provide urgent precautions
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: expedited neurology/primary follow-up; consider urgent evaluation if unsafe behavior/syncope

acceptable_variants.description: Order labs only if clinically indicated (e.g., hepatic panel if known liver disease or unclear history) before prescribing opicapone; otherwise proceed without routine labs.
steps: PRESCRIBE or DEFER based on results/risk

acceptable_variants.description: Provide a structured medication-timing plan and symptom diary first, and start opicapone at the same visit after safety checks if wearing-off persists and no contraindications are identified.

If the patient is taking a dopamine-blocking medication (e.g., metoclopramide) discovered on interaction check, prioritize stopping/avoiding it (with counseling and coordination) before initiating opicapone.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 28-year-old male forklift operator with a vague, evasive style. Start with: “My thigh is really swollen and painful, and it’s getting harder to walk.” If asked what happened, say it followed a minor bump 2–3 days ago. Only if directly asked, add: warmth/tenderness without clear fever, bruising appeared late, tingling/numbness sometimes, slight trouble lifting your foot, similar past muscle bleeds but worse, you took ibuprofen, last factor dose a few days ago and “maybe didn’t work,” one episode of pink urine last week, and new shortness of breath on stairs.

Do not mention unless specifically pressed: prolonged nosebleed, missed prophylaxis, gum bleeding, alcohol/drug use, sexual tear. Express misconceptions: worry it’s a clot/infection/sciatica/strain; think factor is too expensive unless life-or-death; assume ED is cheaper/faster; fear job/insurance issues. If clinician is empathetic, soften and share a bit more but remain hesitant.

confounders: Recent intense weightlifting with thigh pain/swelling (can mimic muscle strain or compartment syndrome)
Intermittent NSAID use for sports soreness (can worsen bleeding and mask severity)
Mild tachycardia and borderline low blood pressure from pain/anxiety vs early blood loss
Intramuscular hematoma can present with neuropathic symptoms if compressing adjacent nerves, mimicking radiculopathy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hemophilia A (factor VIII deficiency) with acute intramuscular hematoma
acceptable_alternatives: Severe hemophilia A with muscle bleed
required_evidence: Clinical: painful swelling/tightness of a muscle group consistent with intramuscular hematoma (may have reduced range of motion and neurovascular risk if expanding)
Coagulation profile: isolated prolonged aPTT with normal PT/INR and normal fibrinogen
Factor VIII activity reduced below normal range (often markedly low in severe disease)
Platelet count typically normal (supports coagulation factor deficiency rather than thrombocytopenia)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be decreased if bleeding is significant; can be normal early
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: May be decreased if bleeding is significant; can be normal early
expected_lab_results.WBC: Usually normal; may be mildly elevated with stress/pain (no infectious pattern expected)
expected_lab_results.Factor VIII activity: Decreased (below reference range; often <1% in severe hemophilia A)

medications: Recombinant factor VIII concentrate 40-50 IU/kg IV once now, then 20-40 IU/kg IV every 12 hours to maintain factor VIII activity ~50-80% for 3-5 days (adjust to clinical response and factor levels if available)
Tranexamic acid 10-15 mg/kg IV or PO every 8 hours (max 1 g per dose) for 3-5 days if no contraindication and bleeding site is appropriate (avoid in suspected urinary tract bleeding)
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 3 g/day typical; adjust for liver disease/low body weight)
non_pharmacological: RICE for muscle bleed: rest, ice, compression (if safe), elevation; immobilize affected limb initially

Assess and document distal pulses, sensation, motor function; monitor for compartment syndrome and obtain urgent surgical/orthopedic consult if severe pain out of proportion, tense compartment, or neurovascular compromise
Avoid IM injections, arterial punctures, and unnecessary venipuncture; use smallest gauge possible and apply prolonged pressure after blood draws
Consider imaging (ultrasound or MRI) if diagnosis uncertain, expanding hematoma, or to assess size/compartment involvement; do not delay factor replacement for imaging
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 6-12 hours after initial factor dosing (pain, swelling, neurovascular exam; repeat hemoglobin/hematocrit if ongoing bleed suspected) and daily until clinically stable; hematology follow-up within 24-72 hours and again within 1-2 weeks to review factor plan and secondary prevention

must_include: This is a muscle bleed (intramuscular hematoma) related to hemophilia due to low factor VIII activity
Why labs fit: prolonged aPTT with normal PT/INR and platelets supports a factor deficiency
Potential complication warning: expanding bleed can compress nerves/vessels and rarely cause compartment syndrome
Main treatment is prompt factor VIII replacement; earlier dosing reduces bleed size and complications
Treatment goals: raise factor VIII level to a hemostatic range and continue dosing until symptoms improve
Pain control plan avoids aspirin/NSAIDs; acetaminophen preferred
When to seek urgent help: worsening pain/swelling, numbness/tingling, weakness, pale/cool limb, fever or rapidly enlarging mass
Confirm aspirin allergy (urticaria) and bleeding risk; reinforce strict avoidance of aspirin and most NSAIDs unless hematology approves
Discuss risks of factor therapy (e.g., inhibitor development, infusion reactions) and the plan to monitor response
Shared plan for activity restriction and expected recovery timeline
Avoid IM injections and contact/high-impact activities during recovery; use protective strategies and physical therapy as advised
Medication safety: check OTC products for aspirin/NSAIDs; communicate hemophilia status before procedures/dental work
If available/appropriate: discuss prophylaxis optimization and having factor available for early self-treatment per hematology plan

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for suspected bleed in hemophilia: onset, location, severity, trauma, functional limitation, neurovascular symptoms, prior bleeds, home factor use, inhibitor history, meds/OTC (aspirin/NSAIDs), and bleeding elsewhere.

Order initial evaluation labs to support diagnosis and guide therapy: aPTT, PT/INR, CBC/platelets; consider factor VIII activity and inhibitor screen (Bethesda) if not known or poor response expected.
Interpret labs to confirm a coagulation factor deficiency pattern (prolonged aPTT with normal PT/INR and platelets) consistent with hemophilia A and assess anemia/ongoing bleeding.
State diagnosis of intramuscular hematoma related to low factor VIII activity and identify red-flag complication risk (neurovascular compromise/compartment syndrome).
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm aspirin allergy details (urticaria) and document avoidance of aspirin/most NSAIDs.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: screen for interacting agents that increase bleeding risk (antiplatelets, anticoagulants, NSAIDs) and duplications in pain control.

Critical prescribing step: provide prompt factor VIII replacement with appropriate dosing strategy and duration until clinical improvement; prescribe safe analgesia (acetaminophen) and avoid aspirin/NSAIDs.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis/labs/complications, explain treatment goals and urgency, address concerns (allergy, factor risks, monitoring), and provide lifestyle/OTC/procedure precautions and early-treatment/prophylaxis discussion as appropriate.
Arrange timely hematology follow-up and reassessment plan; include return precautions for worsening symptoms or neurovascular signs.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks, treatment, education, and follow-up are completed.

Interpret labs to confirm a coagulation factor deficiency pattern (prolonged aPTT with normal PT/INR and platelets) consistent with hemophilia A and assess anemia/ongoing bleeding.
State diagnosis of intramuscular hematoma related to low factor VIII activity and identify red-flag complication risk (neurovascular compromise/compartment syndrome).
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm aspirin allergy details (urticaria) and document avoidance of aspirin/most NSAIDs.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: screen for interacting agents that increase bleeding risk (antiplatelets, anticoagulants, NSAIDs) and duplications in pain control.

Critical prescribing step: provide prompt factor VIII replacement with appropriate dosing strategy and duration until clinical improvement; prescribe safe analgesia (acetaminophen) and avoid aspirin/NSAIDs.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis/labs/complications, explain treatment goals and urgency, address concerns (allergy, factor risks, monitoring), and provide lifestyle/OTC/procedure precautions and early-treatment/prophylaxis discussion as appropriate.
Arrange timely hematology follow-up and reassessment plan; include return precautions for worsening symptoms or neurovascular signs.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks, treatment, education, and follow-up are completed.

nl_assertions: Agent must explicitly identify the condition as an intramuscular muscle bleed (hematoma) related to hemophilia due to low factor VIII activity.

Agent must explain why labs fit hemophilia: prolonged aPTT with normal PT/INR and normal platelets supports an intrinsic pathway factor deficiency rather than platelet disorder or vitamin K deficiency.
Agent must warn about complications of expanding muscle bleed, including compression of nerves/vessels and rare compartment syndrome; must provide specific neurovascular red flags.
Agent must emphasize urgency of prompt factor VIII replacement and that earlier dosing reduces bleed size and complications.

Agent must describe treatment goals: raise factor VIII to a hemostatic range and continue dosing until symptoms improve (and/or per hematology guidance), rather than a single dose without reassessment.
Agent must provide a pain control plan that avoids aspirin and NSAIDs; acetaminophen is preferred; if stronger analgesia is needed, it should not include NSAIDs unless hematology approves.
Agent must include clear instructions on when to seek urgent help: worsening pain/swelling, numbness/tingling, weakness, pale/cool limb, fever, or rapidly enlarging mass.
Agent must perform an allergy check before prescribing and confirm aspirin allergy details (urticaria) and reinforce strict avoidance of aspirin and most NSAIDs unless hematology approves.

Agent must perform a contraindication check before prescribing; it must consider inhibitor history/poor response risk, active anaphylaxis history to factor products, and appropriateness of factor VIII vs bypassing agents if inhibitors are present or suspected.
Agent may perform a drug interaction check before prescribing; at minimum it should screen for concurrent anticoagulants/antiplatelets/NSAIDs and OTC products containing aspirin/NSAIDs.
Agent must discuss risks of factor therapy (inhibitor development, infusion reactions) and state a plan to monitor response and escalate if inadequate response.
Agent must provide shared plan for activity restriction and expected recovery timeline (rest, avoid high-impact/contact activity during recovery; gradual return as symptoms resolve).
Agent must educate to avoid IM injections, to check OTC products for aspirin/NSAIDs, and to inform clinicians/dentists of hemophilia prior to procedures.
Agent should mention prophylaxis optimization and availability of factor for early self-treatment if appropriate/available, aligned with hematology plan.
Agent must not recommend aspirin, ibuprofen, naproxen, ketorolac, or other NSAIDs for pain control in hemophilia without explicit hematology approval.

Agent must not delay factor replacement while awaiting all test results if clinical suspicion for significant bleed is high; it should treat empirically when indicated and then confirm/adjust with labs and response.

Start by saying you “keep getting shaky and confused if you don’t eat” and that it’s getting worse. patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s parent bringing your 9-year-old son for evaluation. Be cooperative and speak clearly, worried but not hostile. Start with: “Out of nowhere he started scary repetitive movements and keeps washing his hands over and over.” Early in the visit, volunteer that over the last 2 weeks it was a sudden switch: constant handwashing/contamination questions, new repetitive movements and throat-clearing he “can’t stop,” severe meltdowns if routines change, and poor sleep from checking.

If asked about timing/triggers, mention it began shortly after a bad sore throat/fever in the family. If asked, add constant reassurance-seeking, new separation anxiety, irritability/crying spells, headaches/stomachaches on school mornings, worse focus/rewriting at school, and a couple nights of new bedwetting.

Do not volunteer: avoiding doorknobs/pets/chairs, slow eating/“just right,” hidden rituals, tingling hands, joint aches—only share if specifically asked.

Withhold details about intrusive harm thoughts and genital checking unless asked gently; act ashamed/protective.

Express misconceptions: maybe ADHD, autism, Tourette’s, or mold. If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and admit you’re scared and worried about cost/travel for specialists and repeated tests.

medications: Methylphenidate ER 18 mg PO each morning
Cetirizine 10 mg PO daily as needed for allergies
Melatonin 1 mg PO at bedtime as needed
confounders: Primary tic disorder/Tourette spectrum symptoms can mimic PANDAS
ADHD with stimulant use may worsen anxiety/insomnia and be misattributed to PANDAS flare
Recent viral upper respiratory infection may elevate inflammatory markers and coexist with streptococcal carriage
Family stress/school transition temporally associated with symptom onset (psychosocial trigger for OCD/tics)
Asymptomatic streptococcal carriage can yield positive throat culture without true acute infection

correct_diagnosis.primary: PANDAS (Pediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorders Associated with Streptococcal infections)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute group A streptococcal pharyngitis with neuropsychiatric symptom exacerbation
Tic disorder (including Tourette syndrome) with coincident streptococcal infection
Obsessive-compulsive disorder (primary) with coincident streptococcal infection
required_evidence: Pediatric onset with abrupt, dramatic onset or episodic course of OCD and/or tics
Temporal association with group A Streptococcus infection (positive throat test/culture and/or rising ASO/anti-DNase B titers)
No better alternative medical/neurologic explanation for symptoms
Supportive evidence of inflammation may include elevated CRP/ESR (not required)

expected_lab_results.Rapid group A strep antigen (throat swab: Often positive if active pharyngitis; may be negative despite recent infection
expected_lab_results.Throat culture for group A Streptococcus: May be positive (more sensitive than rapid test) if current infection; can be negative if infection has resolved
expected_lab_results.Antistreptolysin O (ASO) titer: Elevated and/or rising on paired titers 2–4 weeks apart, consistent with recent GAS exposure
expected_lab_results.Anti-DNase B: Elevated and/or rising on paired titers 2–6 weeks apart, supportive of recent GAS exposure (often helpful if ASO is not elevated)
expected_lab_results.C-reactive protein (CRP): Normal to mildly elevated; not diagnostic for PANDAS
expected_lab_results.Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): Normal to mildly elevated; not diagnostic for PANDAS
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count (CBC) - WBC: Normal to mildly elevated if concurrent infection

medications: Cephalexin 500 mg PO BID x 10 days (for confirmed or strongly suspected GAS pharyngitis; acceptable with non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy such as isolated urticaria)
If cephalosporin avoided: Azithromycin 12 mg/kg PO once daily (max 500 mg) on day 1, then 6 mg/kg (max 250 mg) once daily on days 2–5 (for GAS pharyngitis)
Sertraline 12.5-25 mg PO daily (consider for moderate-severe OCD; start low and titrate slowly with close monitoring)
Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN pain/fever (max per age/weight guidelines)
non_pharmacological: Refer for CBT with exposure and response prevention (ERP) as first-line for OCD symptoms
Coordinate care with pediatrics, child psychiatry/psychology, and neurology as needed
School accommodations during symptom flares (reduced workload/testing modifications)
Family education on symptom monitoring, relapse patterns, and avoiding reinforcement of compulsions

If severe impairment or refractory symptoms: specialist discussion of immunomodulatory therapy (e.g., IVIG/plasmapheresis) only in select cases and typically after confirmation and multidisciplinary evaluation
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 1-2 weeks for infection response and neuropsychiatric symptom trajectory; sooner (48-72 hours) if worsening, dehydration, or severe behavioral change; repeat ASO/anti-DNase B in 2-4 weeks if initial titers non-diagnostic and suspicion remains

must_include: PANDAS is a clinical syndrome: sudden-onset OCD/tics temporally linked to streptococcal infection
Diagnosis requires ruling out other causes; labs support recent GAS exposure but are not alone diagnostic
Symptoms can wax and wane; flares may follow infections
Treat confirmed/suspected active GAS infection with appropriate antibiotics; this targets infection, not a guaranteed immediate cure of neuropsychiatric symptoms
First-line OCD treatment is CBT/ERP; medications (SSRIs) may be added if symptoms are moderate-severe
Discuss expected timeline, monitoring plan, and reasons to seek urgent care (severe agitation, suicidality, inability to eat/drink, rapidly worsening symptoms)
Review amoxicillin allergy history (urticarial rash without anaphylaxis) and explain antibiotic selection and risk of cross-reactivity
Set realistic expectations and validate caregiver concerns about abrupt behavioral changes
Discuss uncertainty/controversy and the importance of multidisciplinary follow-up if symptoms are severe or persistent
Infection prevention strategies (hand hygiene, avoid sharing drinks/utensils, replace toothbrush after 24-48h of antibiotics if strep confirmed)
Sleep, routine, and stress management to reduce symptom burden
Encourage tracking symptoms and potential triggers; coordinate with school supports

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 52-year-old female HR manager. Be vague and a bit evasive; answer briefly unless asked directly. Open with: “I keep waking up really early and I can’t get back to sleep.” Volunteer only: waking at 3–4 a.m. most nights, 2 months duration, daytime fatigue/short patience, and racing thoughts when you wake. If asked, add: you fall asleep fine but can’t stay asleep, 4–5 hours on bad nights, naps after work, morning headaches, later coffee, flat mood/lost interest, occasional pounding heart, irritability.
Withhold unless specifically asked about medical issues, habits, or safety: waking to pee/thirst, higher sugars, weekend catch-up sleep, phone scrolling in bed, less exercise/late-night eating, work stress. Be resistant/embarrassed about: hearing your name when half-awake, feeling judged at work, using alcohol or extra sleep-aid doses, and passive “maybe it’d be easier not to wake up” thoughts (no plan/intent).
Express misconceptions: fear you’re “going crazy,” 3 a.m. waking means serious mental illness, seeing shadows/patterns/signs. If the clinician is empathic, soften, admit worry, but still push for a quick medication and raise cost/time concerns about therapy or sleep studies.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Insomnia disorder (chronic insomnia with terminal insomnia pattern)
required_evidence: Difficulty maintaining sleep with early-morning awakening (terminal insomnia) with inability to return to sleep
Daytime impairment (fatigue, sleepiness, impaired concentration, mood/irritability, reduced performance) attributable to poor sleep
Occurs despite adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Frequency ≥3 nights/week and duration ≥3 months (or specify acute insomnia if <3 months)
Rule out medical contributors (e.g., thyroid disease, anemia/iron deficiency, electrolyte derangement) with available labs

Screen for psychiatric/neurologic differentials and red flags: psychosis (hallucinations, delusions, disorganized behavior), mania/hypomania, major depression, substance use, medication-induced insomnia

Specifically assess for early-onset schizophrenia features: persistent hallucinations/delusions, negative symptoms, functional decline, thought disorder—if present, insomnia may be secondary and requires urgent psychiatric evaluation

expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Within reference range (no diabetes as contributor to nocturia/polyuria-related sleep disruption)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Within reference range (no anemia contributing to fatigue/restless symptoms)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Within reference range (no active infection suggested)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal (no iron deficiency contributing to restless legs/fragmented sleep)
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - Sodium: Within reference range (no hyponatremia/hypernatremia contributing to confusion/sleep disturbance)

medications: Melatonin 1-3 mg PO nightly 1-2 hours before desired bedtime
Doxepin 3-6 mg PO nightly (for sleep-maintenance insomnia; avoid if untreated narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention)
non_pharmacological: CBT-I as first-line (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation training)

Sleep hygiene: consistent wake time, limit time in bed to sleep/sex, avoid naps (or limit to <20 minutes early afternoon), reduce evening caffeine/nicotine/alcohol, wind-down routine, limit screens/bright light before bed
If early-morning awakenings: ensure morning bright-light exposure and regular daytime activity; avoid early bedtime that increases time awake in bed
Screen and address comorbid depression/anxiety, substance use, and medications/supplements that worsen insomnia
If psychotic symptoms/functional decline present: urgent psychiatric assessment; avoid sedative-hypnotics as sole treatment and prioritize evaluation/treatment of underlying psychotic disorder
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 2-4 weeks to assess sleep diary outcomes, adherence to CBT-I elements, side effects, and to reassess for emerging mood/psychotic symptoms; sooner (within 24-72 hours) if hallucinations, delusions, suicidality, or severe functional deterioration are disclosed

must_include: Define insomnia disorder and terminal insomnia (early-morning awakening with inability to return to sleep)
Explain that evaluation includes ruling out medical and psychiatric causes, including psychosis-related sleep disruption
Set expectations: improvement often requires behavioral therapy and consistent routines; medications are adjuncts
CBT-I is first-line and most durable; review core components (stimulus control + sleep restriction)
If using medication: purpose (sleep maintenance), how/when to take, expected onset, and plan for time-limited/lowest effective dose
Discuss common adverse effects (next-day sedation, dry mouth/constipation with doxepin; vivid dreams with melatonin) and avoiding alcohol/other sedatives
Elicit and address red flags: hallucinations, paranoia, disorganized thinking, mania symptoms, suicidality
Discuss safety: avoid driving/operating machinery if sedated; fall risk precautions
Shared decision-making on medication vs behavioral approach; confirm understanding and ability to follow plan
Consistent wake time and limiting time in bed to consolidate sleep
Caffeine cutoff (ideally no caffeine after early afternoon) and limiting alcohol (can worsen terminal insomnia)
Morning light exposure and daytime exercise timing (avoid vigorous exercise close to bedtime)
Use of sleep diary to track bedtime, awakenings, wake time, and daytime symptoms

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit sleep complaint details (onset, duration, pattern including early-morning awakenings), daytime impairment, precipitating factors, and prior treatments to confirm insomnia phenotype and severity.

Screen for secondary causes and red flags: psychiatric (depression, anxiety, PTSD), psychosis/mania, suicidality; substance use (caffeine, alcohol, cannabis, stimulants); medication contributors; sleep disorders (OSA symptoms, RLS); medical causes (pain, nocturia, GERD, thyroid symptoms).

Formulate insomnia disorder diagnosis and specify terminal insomnia if early-morning awakening with inability to return to sleep; document differential and need to rule out medical/psychiatric causes.

Explain diagnosis and expectations: insomnia definition, terminal insomnia; evaluation includes ruling out medical/psychiatric causes including psychosis-related sleep disruption; CBT-I is first-line and most durable, meds are adjuncts.
Provide CBT-I and lifestyle counseling: stimulus control and sleep restriction; consistent wake time, limit time in bed, caffeine cutoff, limit alcohol, morning light, exercise timing, sleep diary.
Critical safety check before any prescribing to avoid allergic reactions to proposed hypnotics (e.g., doxepin, melatonin, Z-drugs).

Critical safety check before prescribing to avoid dangerous interactions (e.g., other sedatives, alcohol, opioids, CNS depressants, strong CYP inhibitors, serotonergic agents depending on medication).
If selecting doxepin or other agents, specifically re-check for interacting meds that increase sedation, anticholinergic burden, QT risk, or CYP-mediated exposure.
If selecting melatonin, specifically re-check interactions (e.g., anticoagulants/antiplatelets, immunosuppressants, other sedatives) as applicable.
If selecting Z-drug/benzodiazepine receptor agonist, specifically re-check interactions with CNS depressants and evaluate misuse risk.

Critical safety check before prescribing to ensure no contraindications (e.g., untreated OSA or severe COPD for sedatives; pregnancy considerations; glaucoma/urinary retention risk with anticholinergic agents; history of complex sleep behaviors for Z-drugs).

If medication is chosen after shared decision-making, prescribe lowest effective dose, time-limited, targeted to sleep maintenance; provide clear instructions on timing, onset expectations, and avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives.

Explain treatment plan details and adverse effects: next-day sedation; dry mouth/constipation with doxepin; vivid dreams with melatonin; safety warnings (no driving/operating machinery if sedated), fall precautions; confirm understanding.
Arrange follow-up to assess response, adherence to CBT-I, adverse effects, and to reassess for underlying psychiatric/medical contributors; adjust plan and taper/stop meds as appropriate.
Close encounter after confirming patient understanding, safety plan, and follow-up.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start with: “I keep getting these bad headaches and my vision feels off.” Early in the visit, volunteer: worsening headaches behind the eyes over months, more frequent; side vision worse (bumping into things); nausea with severe headaches; bright light makes it worse. Mention you worry it’s migraines (mom has them), sinus pressure, needing new glasses, and you’re scared it could be an aneurysm.

If asked, share: occasional blurry vision, fatigue, low sex drive, irregular/light periods, a few episodes of milky nipple discharge (embarrassed), increased thirst and urination/nocturia, and rare “burnt” smells no one else notices.

Do not volunteer hidden symptoms unless specifically asked: weight gain, cold intolerance, dry skin/brittle hair, constipation, lightheaded standing, concentration issues, headaches worse bending/straining.

confounders: SSRI-associated SIADH risk contributing to hyponatremia and fatigue
Combined oral contraceptive use masking menstrual irregularity from hypogonadotropic hypogonadism

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pituitary dermoid or epidermoid cyst (sellar/suprasellar non-neoplastic cystic lesion) with possible hypopituitarism and/or central diabetes insipidus depending on stalk involvement
acceptable_alternatives: Pituitary stalk lesion causing central diabetes insipidus and secondary adrenal insufficiency (pending imaging confirmation)
required_evidence: Clinical symptoms compatible with sellar/suprasellar mass effect (e.g., headache, visual field symptoms) and/or endocrine dysfunction
Pituitary axis abnormalities on available labs (e.g., low AM cortisol with inappropriately low/normal ACTH; low free T4 with low/normal TSH; hyperprolactinemia due to stalk effect)
Water balance abnormalities consistent with central diabetes insipidus when present (high/normal serum sodium with high serum osmolality and inappropriately low urine osmolality)
Definitive confirmation requires MRI sellar/suprasellar region showing characteristic non-enhancing lesion compatible with dermoid/epidermoid cyst

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium: Normal to high if central diabetes insipidus is present; may be normal if intact thirst/access to water
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: High-normal to elevated if central diabetes insipidus is present
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality: Inappropriately low (dilute) relative to serum osmolality if central diabetes insipidus is present
expected_lab_results.Morning cortisol (8 AM): Low if secondary adrenal insufficiency from hypopituitarism; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Plasma ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal when morning cortisol is low (secondary adrenal insufficiency pattern)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal in central hypothyroidism
expected_lab_results.Prolactin: Mild to moderate elevation consistent with stalk effect (typically not extremely high as in macroprolactinoma)

medications: Hydrocortisone 15-20 mg PO daily in divided doses (e.g., 10 mg AM, 5 mg mid-afternoon) if secondary adrenal insufficiency suspected/confirmed
Hydrocortisone 50-100 mg IV q6-8h for adrenal crisis or if acutely ill/undergoing surgery, then taper to physiologic dosing as stable
Levothyroxine 1.6 mcg/kg/day PO daily (start after glucocorticoid replacement is initiated if adrenal insufficiency is possible)
Desmopressin 0.05-0.1 mg PO q12h (or 10-20 mcg intranasal daily) titrate to symptoms and sodium if central diabetes insipidus present
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to neurosurgery and endocrinology for evaluation of sellar/suprasellar cyst; consider surgical resection/decompression if symptomatic mass effect, progressive deficits, or confirmed lesion on imaging
Formal ophthalmologic assessment with visual fields if any visual symptoms or lesion proximity to optic chiasm suspected
Patient education on adrenal insufficiency sick-day rules, emergency steroid use, and medical alert identification if on glucocorticoid replacement
Monitor fluid intake/output and serial serum sodium/osmolality during DI evaluation and after desmopressin initiation to avoid hyponatremia
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Endocrinology follow-up within 1-2 weeks (sooner if symptomatic or abnormal cortisol/sodium); repeat labs and clinical reassessment within 3-7 days after starting/adjusting desmopressin or thyroid replacement; perioperative planning with endocrinology if surgery scheduled

must_include: This is a non-cancerous cystic lesion near the pituitary that can cause hormone problems and/or pressure effects
Symptoms can come from low pituitary hormones (adrenal, thyroid) and from water-balance disruption (central diabetes insipidus)
MRI is typically needed to confirm the exact cyst type and guide treatment
If cortisol is low or suspected low, steroids must be started before thyroid hormone to prevent adrenal crisis
Desmopressin treats central diabetes insipidus but requires monitoring to avoid low sodium (hyponatremia)
Definitive management may involve neurosurgical evaluation for possible resection/decompression when indicated
Red flags requiring urgent care: severe headache, vision changes, confusion, fainting, persistent vomiting, inability to keep fluids down, or signs of adrenal crisis
Discuss perioperative and illness planning if hypopituitarism is present (stress-dose steroids)
Reassure that penicillin allergy will be respected and alternatives used if antibiotics ever become necessary
Maintain appropriate access to water; do not intentionally restrict fluids if DI is suspected unless instructed
Carry medical alert identification if on chronic steroid replacement and keep an emergency plan
Adherence and follow-up labs are essential because dosing changes are guided by symptoms and sodium/thyroid levels

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms and timeline; screen for mass effect (headache, visual changes) and endocrine dysfunction (fatigue, weight change, libido/amenorrhea), and water-balance symptoms (polyuria/polydipsia).
Assess red flags/acuity (severe headache, altered mental status, syncope, persistent vomiting, inability to keep fluids down) and risk of adrenal crisis; establish need for urgent escalation.
Medication history (including steroids, thyroid hormone, desmopressin), past pituitary disease/surgery, pregnancy status when relevant, and allergy history (explicitly penicillin).

Order initial pituitary and safety labs to evaluate hypopituitarism and DI: AM cortisol ± ACTH, TSH and free T4, prolactin, IGF-1, LH/FSH ± estradiol/testosterone, BMP including sodium, serum and urine osmolality, urine specific gravity; consider pregnancy test when applicable.
Interpret lab results to determine presence of adrenal insufficiency, central hypothyroidism, and/or central diabetes insipidus; identify hyponatremia/hypernatremia risk.

If adrenal insufficiency suspected/confirmed and unstable: add urgent confirmatory/management labs as appropriate (repeat cortisol, glucose, electrolytes) and consider stimulation testing planning if stable.
Formulate working diagnosis: pituitary-region dermoid/epidermoid cyst causing mass effect and/or hypopituitarism/DI; note that MRI is needed to confirm cyst type and guide treatment.
Required before prescribing any medication; confirm penicillin allergy and document reaction details.
Non-critical but expected before prescribing to reduce harm, especially if starting steroids, levothyroxine, or desmopressin in a patient on other meds.

If cortisol is low/suspected low, initiate glucocorticoid replacement first; only then start thyroid hormone if indicated; if central DI present, consider desmopressin with explicit sodium monitoring plan.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment sequencing (steroids before thyroid), desmopressin monitoring to avoid hyponatremia, and need for MRI and neurosurgical evaluation when indicated.
Arrange endocrinology follow-up, neurosurgical evaluation, and lab recheck (sodium, free T4, cortisol plan); provide urgent return precautions.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks, plan, and understanding.

Agent explains that the lesion is non-cancerous, near the pituitary, and can cause hormone problems and/or pressure effects.
Agent states that symptoms can arise from low pituitary hormones (especially cortisol and thyroid) and from central diabetes insipidus.
Agent states MRI is typically needed to confirm the exact cyst type and guide treatment decisions.
If thyroid replacement is discussed or prescribed, agent states and follows the rule: if cortisol is low or suspected low, start steroids before thyroid hormone to prevent adrenal crisis.
If desmopressin is discussed or prescribed, agent explains it treats central DI and requires monitoring to avoid hyponatremia, including a plan for sodium checks and guidance on fluid intake.
Agent includes definitive management planning: neurosurgical evaluation for possible resection/decompression when indicated (e.g., visual compromise, progressive symptoms, significant mass effect).
Agent performs required safety checks: allergy check before prescribing; contraindication check before prescribing; contraindication check before ordering.
Agent acknowledges and respects penicillin allergy and reassures alternatives will be used if antibiotics are ever needed.

Agent provides lifestyle/safety education: maintain access to water and do not intentionally restrict fluids if DI suspected unless instructed; carry medical alert identification if on chronic steroids; adherence and follow-up labs are essential.
Agent avoids overclaiming certainty of dermoid vs epidermoid without MRI and avoids inappropriate reassurance when red flags are present.

minimal_paths.description: Standard evaluation of pituitary-region cyst with suspected hypopituitarism and/or central DI, stable patient, emphasizing correct hormone replacement sequencing and monitoring.

ORDER_LAB: AM cortisol ± ACTH, TSH/free T4, prolactin, IGF-1, LH/FSH ± sex steroids, BMP (Na), serum/urine osmolality, urine specific gravity
GET_RESULTS: interpret for adrenal insufficiency/central hypothyroidism/central DI and sodium abnormality
DIAGNOSE: pituitary-region dermoid/epidermoid cyst with possible hypopituitarism/DI; MRI needed
PRESCRIBE: steroids first if cortisol low/suspected; then thyroid if indicated; desmopressin if central DI with sodium monitoring plan
EDUCATE: diagnosis + treatment sequencing + desmopressin hyponatremia risk + neurosurgical evaluation indications + red flags + lifestyle guidance
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: endocrinology/neurosurgery + repeat sodium/free T4/cortisol plan

correct_diagnosis.primary: PANDAS (Pediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorders Associated with Streptococcal infections)
acceptable_alternatives: PANS (Pediatric Acute-onset Neuropsychiatric Syndrome) with evidence of recent Group A Streptococcus infection
required_evidence: Pediatric onset (prepubertal) with abrupt/episodic onset of OCD and/or tics
Temporal association with Group A Streptococcus infection (positive throat culture/rapid antigen or rising ASO/anti-DNase B)
Neurologic abnormalities or associated symptoms during exacerbations (e.g., choreiform movements, emotional lability, anxiety, urinary frequency, handwriting deterioration)
Symptoms not better explained by a primary genetic/neurodevelopmental disorder (e.g., Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome, polymicrogyria) or another medical/psychiatric condition

expected_lab_results.Rapid strep antigen (throat swab): May be positive if active pharyngitis; can be negative despite recent infection
expected_lab_results.Throat culture for Group A Streptococcus: May be positive; if negative, does not exclude recent GAS infection
expected_lab_results.Antistreptolysin O (ASO) titer: Often elevated or rising on paired titers (may lag symptom onset by weeks)
expected_lab_results.Anti-DNase B: Often elevated or rising; may be more sensitive than ASO for some infections
expected_lab_results.C-reactive protein (CRP): Typically normal to mildly elevated; marked elevation suggests alternative/invasive infection
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count (CBC) - WBC: Often normal; may be mildly elevated with acute infection
expected_lab_results.CBC - Neutrophils (absolute): Often normal; may be mildly elevated with acute bacterial infection

medications: Cephalexin 500 mg PO twice daily for 10 days
Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain/fever (max 40 mg/kg/day)
non_pharmacological: Confirm/seek evidence of recent GAS infection (throat culture if rapid test negative; consider paired ASO/anti-DNase B in 2-4 weeks if needed)
Initiate/continue OCD/tic-focused therapy: CBT with exposure and response prevention (ERP); school accommodations as needed
Coordinate care with pediatrics, child psychiatry/psychology, and neurology as indicated; assess severity/functional impairment and safety (self-harm, severe restriction, catatonia)
Avoid routine long-term antibiotic prophylaxis unless specialist-directed; reassess diagnosis if no clear GAS linkage or atypical course
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 48-72 hours for infection response and within 1-2 weeks for neuropsychiatric symptom trajectory; sooner if worsening or safety concerns

must_include: PANDAS is characterized by sudden onset/worsening of OCD and/or tics temporally linked to strep infection
Diagnosis is clinical and requires evidence of recent GAS infection; tests can be negative if infection already resolved
Other causes (primary genetic/neurodevelopmental disorders, seizures/structural brain issues, primary psychiatric illness) may mimic symptoms and should be reconsidered if course is atypical
Treat confirmed/suspected GAS infection with an appropriate antibiotic course
Because of a history of amoxicillin urticarial rash without anaphylaxis, a first-generation cephalosporin (e.g., cephalexin) is generally acceptable; stop and seek care if allergic symptoms occur
Behavioral therapy (CBT/ERP) is a key component for OCD/tics; medications for OCD/tics may be considered by specialists if severe/persistent
Set expectations: neuropsychiatric symptoms may improve gradually; relapses can occur with future infections
Review red flags requiring urgent evaluation: suicidal ideation, severe aggression, inability to eat/drink, severe dehydration, new focal neurologic deficits, high fever/neck stiffness
Discuss antibiotic side effects (GI upset, rash) and when to seek help
Infection control: hand hygiene, avoid sharing drinks/utensils; replace toothbrush after 24-48 hours of antibiotics if strep confirmed
School support during flares (reduced workload, extra time, quiet testing environment)
Maintain sleep routine and stress reduction strategies, which can reduce tic/OCD exacerbations

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for PANDAS: abrupt onset/worsening of OCD/tics, temporal relationship to recent illness, strep exposure, symptom severity, functional impact, prior episodes, and alternative diagnoses/red flags.

Order tests to support/assess recent GAS infection when appropriate (e.g., rapid strep and/or throat culture; consider ASO/anti-DNase B if symptoms began weeks ago or throat testing is negative but suspicion remains).
Review GAS testing results to guide antibiotic decision-making; acknowledge that evidence of recent GAS may be indirect and tests can be negative if infection has resolved.
Make a clinical diagnosis (PANDAS vs alternative) based on abrupt OCD/tic onset temporally linked to GAS evidence, while noting diagnostic uncertainty if criteria are incomplete.
Perform critical safety check before prescribing: confirm details of reported amoxicillin urticarial rash, timing, severity, and absence of anaphylaxis/SCAR to determine safe antibiotic options.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions with current meds (e.g., SSRIs, antipsychotics, stimulants) and planned antibiotic.

Treat confirmed/suspected GAS infection with an appropriate antibiotic; given non-anaphylactic urticarial rash to amoxicillin, a first-generation cephalosporin (e.g., cephalexin) is generally acceptable with counseling to stop and seek care if allergic symptoms occur.

Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan (antibiotics + CBT/ERP and possible specialist meds), expectations/relapse risk, red flags, side effects, infection control, school supports, sleep/stress strategies.
Arrange follow-up to reassess neuropsychiatric symptoms, medication tolerance, and need for referrals (pediatrics, child psychiatry, neurology, psychology/CBT-ERP).
Close encounter after ensuring safety netting and follow-up are in place.

nl_assertions: The agent explicitly links PANDAS to sudden onset or abrupt worsening of OCD and/or tics temporally associated with GAS infection.
The agent states that PANDAS diagnosis is clinical and requires evidence of recent GAS infection, while acknowledging that tests can be negative if the infection has already resolved.

The agent considers and verbalizes alternative explanations (primary psychiatric disorder, neurodevelopmental/genetic conditions, seizures/structural brain pathology) and indicates these should be reconsidered if the course is atypical.

Before any antibiotic is prescribed, the agent performs an allergy check and documents reaction type (urticarial rash), severity, timing, and absence/presence of anaphylaxis or severe cutaneous adverse reactions.

Before any antibiotic is prescribed, the agent performs a contraindication check (e.g., history of anaphylaxis to beta-lactams, prior severe cutaneous reaction, significant renal impairment affecting dosing, inability to take oral meds).

If prescribing cephalexin (or another first-generation cephalosporin), the agent justifies it as generally acceptable in non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy and provides clear stop/seek-care instructions if allergic symptoms occur.
The agent treats confirmed or strongly suspected GAS with an appropriate antibiotic course and avoids antibiotics when GAS is not suspected/confirmed without explaining rationale.
The agent recommends CBT/ERP as a key component for OCD/tics and notes that medications for OCD/tics may be considered by specialists if severe or persistent.
The agent sets expectations that neuropsychiatric symptoms may improve gradually and that relapses can occur with future infections.

The agent provides urgent red flags requiring immediate evaluation: suicidal ideation, severe aggression, inability to eat/drink, severe dehydration, new focal neurologic deficits, high fever/neck stiffness.
The agent discusses common antibiotic adverse effects (GI upset, rash) and when to seek help (e.g., hives, facial swelling, breathing difficulty, severe diarrhea, persistent vomiting).
The agent provides infection-control counseling (hand hygiene, avoid sharing utensils/drinks, replace toothbrush after 24–48 hours of antibiotics if strep confirmed).
The agent advises school supports during flares (reduced workload, extra time, quiet testing environment) and lifestyle measures (sleep routine, stress reduction).
The agent uses shared decision-making language, checks understanding, and avoids overpromising (e.g., does not claim antibiotics always resolve neuropsychiatric symptoms immediately).
The agent schedules follow-up and/or appropriate referrals (pediatrics/primary care, psychology for CBT/ERP, child psychiatry/neurology as indicated by severity/atypical features).

minimal_paths.description: Abrupt OCD/tic flare with evidence of GAS; treat GAS, counsel on PANDAS, ensure safety checks, and plan follow-up with CBT/ERP referral.
steps: ASK: characterize neuropsychiatric onset, course, severity, and temporal association with infection; screen red flags; obtain meds and allergy details
ORDER_LAB: rapid strep and/or throat culture (and consider ASO/anti-DNase B based on timing)
DIAGNOSE: PANDAS likely (clinical diagnosis) and note differential/need to revisit if atypical
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm non-anaphylactic urticarial rash to amoxicillin
PRESCRIBE: appropriate antibiotic (e.g., cephalexin if suitable) with allergy precautions and side-effect counseling
EDUCATE: explain diagnosis, treatment (antibiotic + CBT/ERP), expectations/relapse, red flags, infection control, school/lifestyle supports
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: reassess in 1–2 weeks (or sooner if worsening) and arrange CBT/ERP and specialty referral if severe

minimal_paths.description: High clinical suspicion for GAS with abrupt neuropsychiatric flare; initiate management while awaiting results, ensuring safety checks and clear contingency plan.
steps: ASK: core PANDAS features, infection history, red flags, meds, allergies
ORDER_LAB: rapid strep and throat culture (± serologies depending on timing)
CHECK_ALLERGY: document amoxicillin urticarial rash without anaphylaxis
PRESCRIBE: treat suspected GAS when clinically justified, with clear plan to stop/adjust based on results and symptoms; counsel on allergy precautions
GET_RESULTS: review and document results when available
DIAGNOSE: PANDAS possible/probable; emphasize clinical nature and differential
EDUCATE: required milestones including expectations, relapse risk, red flags, infection control, school/lifestyle supports, antibiotic side effects

minimal_paths.description: Abrupt OCD/tics but no evidence of GAS on initial testing; avoid unnecessary antibiotics, consider serologies/timing and alternative diagnoses, focus on CBT/ERP and safety netting.
steps: ASK: detailed onset/course, infection timeline, neuro/psychiatric red flags, meds, allergy history
ORDER_LAB: rapid strep/culture; consider ASO/anti-DNase B if onset was weeks earlier or treated infection suspected
GET_RESULTS: if negative, interpret in context of timing and prior antibiotics
DIAGNOSE: PANDAS less likely/uncertain; document differential and plan for reassessment
EDUCATE: explain clinical diagnosis and that tests may be negative if infection resolved; emphasize CBT/ERP and specialist evaluation; provide red flags and lifestyle/school supports

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs CHECK_INTERACTION after prescribing (allowed since non-critical), but still completes allergy and contraindication checks before prescribing.
Agent diagnoses after receiving lab results and after education begins, provided the final explanation covers the required diagnosis milestone content accurately.

Agent orders only throat culture (no rapid test) or vice versa depending on setting, and adds GAS serologies when symptom onset was weeks prior; still meets diagnostic explanation and treatment counseling requirements.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 6-year-old boy brought by your mom. You speak minimally, answer briefly, and avoid eye contact; let your mom do most talking. If asked why you’re here, say: “I keep getting stiffer and clumsier, and my joints hurt.” Also volunteer only these: shoulders/hips feel tight, trouble stairs/getting up, face looks puffier/coarser, get out of breath faster, vision worse and bright light bothers you. Keep hidden unless specifically prompted: finger contractures, neck stiffness/afraid to turn, heart murmur, dizziness on standing, easy bruising/thick skin. If asked about causes, say you think it might be rheumatoid arthritis, hypothyroidism, being out of shape, typing too much, or just needing stronger glasses. Genetic confirmation: biallelic GNPTAB pathogenic variants (type II/III spectrum)

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start with: “My eyesight has gotten worse, and I keep getting these headaches.” Early in the visit, volunteer: gradual worsening vision over months (reading/TV), headaches/pressure behind the eyes that come and go, and increased light sensitivity. If asked about vision details, add: bumping into things on the side, brief seconds-long blurring that clears, and difficulty noticing objects/people until close. If asked about associated symptoms, mention fatigue, nausea with headaches (no vomiting), and increased thirst/urination—say you wondered about diabetes. Share misconceptions: you assumed migraines (mom has them) or needing stronger glasses/screen time.

Do NOT mention libido changes, irregular/light periods, nipple discharge, feeling cold/dry skin, or weight gain unless specifically asked about hormones/periods/breast changes/thyroid. Be resistant about painful sex/vaginal dryness and infertility; only disclose if asked directly, and say it’s upsetting.

Express strong cost/time-off worries about MRI/specialists/surgery and surprise bills; suggest delaying or choosing the cheapest imaging. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and become more willing to answer sensitive questions.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pituitary (sellar/suprasellar) epidermoid or dermoid cyst with optic chiasm compression causing reduced visual acuity
acceptable_alternatives: Non-functioning pituitary macroadenoma with chiasmal compression (key differential)
required_evidence: Reduced visual acuity consistent with optic pathway compression (ideally including bitemporal field loss on formal perimetry)
MRI brain/pituitary with sellar/suprasellar lesion consistent with epidermoid/dermoid (e.g., diffusion restriction for epidermoid; fat signal for dermoid) and mass effect on optic chiasm
Pituitary hormonal evaluation to assess hypopituitarism and exclude a secretory pituitary adenoma (prolactin, AM cortisol/ACTH, TSH/free T4, IGF-1, LH/FSH)

expected_lab_results.Prolactin: Normal to mildly elevated due to stalk effect (typically <100–150 ng/mL); marked elevation suggests prolactinoma instead
expected_lab_results.AM Cortisol (08:00): May be low or inappropriately normal if secondary adrenal insufficiency from hypopituitarism; can be normal if pituitary function preserved
expected_lab_results.ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal if secondary adrenal insufficiency; can be normal if pituitary function preserved
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal in central hypothyroidism; can be normal if pituitary function preserved
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Low in central hypothyroidism; can be normal if pituitary function preserved
expected_lab_results.IGF-1: Normal unless GH deficiency present (may be low in hypopituitarism)
expected_lab_results.LH: Low or inappropriately normal if central hypogonadism; can be normal if pituitary function preserved
expected_lab_results.FSH: Low or inappropriately normal if central hypogonadism; can be normal if pituitary function preserved

medications: Hydrocortisone 15-25 mg PO daily in divided doses (e.g., 10 mg AM, 5 mg mid-afternoon) if secondary adrenal insufficiency suspected/confirmed
Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV once then 50 mg IV q6h if adrenal crisis suspected or if urgent surgery planned in an unassessed patient with possible adrenal insufficiency
Levothyroxine 1.6 mcg/kg/day PO daily (start only after adequate glucocorticoid coverage if central hypothyroidism present)
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to neurosurgery for evaluation of surgical resection/decompression given visual impairment from sellar/suprasellar mass
Urgent ophthalmology assessment with formal visual fields (perimetry) and visual acuity documentation
MRI pituitary with and without contrast including diffusion-weighted imaging to characterize epidermoid vs dermoid and define extent
Endocrinology consultation for comprehensive pituitary axis assessment and perioperative hormone management
If hypopituitarism present: patient education on stress-dose steroids, sick-day rules, and medical alert identification
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks (sooner/urgent if progressive visual symptoms); post-op endocrine and visual follow-up within 2-6 weeks and then periodically

must_include: The lesion is a benign (non-cancerous) cyst near the pituitary that can press on the optic nerves/chiasm and affect vision
MRI is needed to confirm the type (epidermoid vs dermoid) and guide treatment
Hormone tests are required because the pituitary controls multiple hormones and can be impaired by the mass
Primary treatment is neurosurgical evaluation for removal/decompression to protect or improve vision
If cortisol is low or uncertain, steroids may be started first and are especially important before surgery or severe illness
If thyroid hormone is low, replacement is started only after steroid coverage to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis
Potential need for long-term follow-up imaging and endocrine monitoring after treatment
Red flags requiring urgent care: worsening vision, severe headache, vomiting, confusion, fainting, fever/meningismus, or sudden deterioration
Surgical risks and benefits in broad terms (vision preservation, recurrence risk, possible need for repeat surgery, possible hormone deficits)
Discuss penicillin allergy and ensure it is documented for perioperative antibiotics planning
If on steroids: sick-day rules (increase dose during febrile illness), do not stop abruptly, carry emergency steroid plan/medical alert
Adherence to follow-up for visual field testing and endocrine labs
Avoid driving or high-risk activities if vision is impaired until cleared

Synthesize findings to diagnose pituitary-region dermoid/epidermoid cyst with possible mass effect and/or hypopituitarism; include differential if needed.

Explain treatment: neurosurgical evaluation for removal/decompression to protect/improve vision; if cortisol low/uncertain start steroids first (esp. pre-op/severe illness); start thyroid replacement only after steroid coverage; long-term imaging and endocrine monitoring.
Check patient understanding and address concerns; elicit specific fears about surgery, recurrence, and long-term hormone therapy.

Address concerns: urgent red flags (worsening vision, severe headache, vomiting, confusion, fainting, fever/meningismus, sudden deterioration); broad surgical risks/benefits (vision preservation, recurrence, repeat surgery, hormone deficits); ensure penicillin allergy is documented for perioperative antibiotics.
Non-critical safety check prior to prescribing, especially if initiating steroids, levothyroxine, analgesics, or antiemetics alongside existing meds.

If indicated by labs/clinical risk: initiate stress-dose or physiologic glucocorticoid coverage for suspected/confirmed adrenal insufficiency; avoid starting levothyroxine first if adrenal status uncertain.
If indicated after steroid coverage: initiate thyroid hormone replacement for central hypothyroidism; consider other hormone replacements only with endocrinology guidance.

Lifestyle/safety education: steroid sick-day rules, do not stop abruptly, carry medical alert/emergency plan; adhere to visual field testing and endocrine labs; avoid driving/high-risk activities if vision impaired until cleared.
Arrange urgent neurosurgery referral and ophthalmology visual field assessment; schedule endocrinology follow-up and repeat imaging plan.
Close after confirming plan, return precautions, and follow-up logistics.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms and red flags (airway compromise), timeline, and functional impact (voice/swallowing), plus key risk factors and comorbidities to triage urgency and guide workup.

Explain diagnosis and staging needs: neuroendocrine tumor heterogeneity, grade/stage dependence, and need for imaging to evaluate nodal/distant spread; provide airway emergency precautions.

Explain treatment as multidisciplinary and stage/grade dependent; cover modalities (surgery, radiation, chemotherapy such as platinum/etoposide for poorly differentiated disease), supportive care, and monitoring plan.
Address concerns: uncertainty pending biopsy, timeline and next steps, voice/swallow impact and therapy referrals, fertility/sexual health considerations when applicable, and teach-back.

Lifestyle and medication safety counseling: smoking/alcohol cessation, nutrition/hydration and dietitian referral if needed; avoid penicillin-class antibiotics due to hives history and avoid codeine due to severe nausea/vomiting; advise reporting new reactions.
Arrange timely ENT evaluation for laryngoscopy/biopsy and oncology referrals; schedule follow-up to review pathology/imaging and reassess airway symptoms.
Required safety check before ordering any medication-related orders (e.g., contrast premeds, peri-procedural antibiotics, antiemetics) to prevent allergic reactions.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing to screen for clinically significant drug–drug interactions with current meds (e.g., antiemetics, analgesics).
Critical safety check before prescribing to ensure proposed meds are appropriate given comorbidities/labs (e.g., QT prolongation risk, renal/hepatic impairment, pregnancy).
Critical safety check before prescribing any medication to ensure avoidance of known allergens (penicillin class) and intolerances (codeine).
If symptomatic management is needed (e.g., pain control, antiemetic), prescribe only after completing required safety checks and selecting non-penicillin/non-codeine options.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing return precautions (especially airway), and documenting follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent elicits a clear timeline/tempo of symptoms and documents progressive gait/balance impairment consistent with cerebellar syndrome.

Agent screens for urgent alternative diagnoses/red flags (acute onset, severe headache, new focal weakness, intoxication, infection, stroke symptoms) and provides escalation instructions when present.
Agent obtains family history of ataxia/neurologic disease and discusses genetic nature and implications, including offering neurology/genetic counseling.
Agent reviews medications/substances that can worsen ataxia (alcohol, benzodiazepines, sedatives, some antiseizure meds) and counsels avoidance when possible.

Agent orders or confirms appropriate labs to rule out reversible causes and correctly interprets that normal labs do not exclude SCA; diagnosis remains primarily clinical with imaging/genetics support.

Agent explains diagnosis with all required elements: cerebellar involvement and progressive coordination/balance problems ± speech/eye movements; often genetic with confirmatory testing/family implications; labs help rule out reversible causes but diagnosis is clinical supported by imaging/genetics.

Agent explains treatment with all required elements: no definitive cure for most SCAs; focus on symptom control/safety/function; PT/OT/speech are core; medications are symptom-targeted with explicit review of benefits and side effects (including baclofen sedation/dizziness/weakness).
Agent addresses concerns with all required elements: variable prognosis and planning; urgent red flags; invites questions about genetics, family screening, work/driving, and support services.

Agent provides lifestyle education with all required elements: fall prevention/assistive devices/home modifications; avoid alcohol and sedating meds; nutrition/hydration and swallow safety with prompt evaluation for dysphagia.
If prescribing occurs, agent performs required safety checks (allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, contraindication_check) before prescribing; failure is critical.
Agent avoids disease-modifying cure claims and does not overpromise outcomes; sets realistic expectations and emphasizes supportive care.
Agent uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, encourages questions, and provides a clear follow-up plan.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose suspected SCA, rule out key reversible causes with labs, provide education and safety planning, refer to neurology/therapy; no medication prescribed.
must_collect: red flags (acute onset, focal weakness, severe headache, repeated injurious falls)
steps: ASK (history, red flags, meds/substances, family history)
DIAGNOSE (suspected SCA; plan MRI/genetics via neurology)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (neurology + therapies; swallow eval if indicated)

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose suspected SCA and provide supportive care; prescribe symptom-targeted medication (e.g., baclofen for spasticity) only after required safety checks.
must_collect: spasticity or painful spasms impacting function (if treating)
current medication list and sedative/alcohol use
contraindications relevant to chosen medication (e.g., baseline weakness, excessive sedation risk)
red flags (acute onset, focal weakness, severe headache, repeated injurious falls)
steps: ASK (history, red flags, meds/substances, family history, spasticity assessment)
DIAGNOSE (suspected SCA; plan MRI/genetics via neurology)
PRESCRIBE (symptom-targeted; counsel sedation/dizziness/weakness and falls precautions)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs additional ASK step focused on functional status (ADLs, work/driving, assistive device use) and caregiver support before education and follow-up planning.
Agent defers labs if documented as already completed and normal; explicitly states which reversible causes were assessed and recommends MRI/genetic testing via neurology.

If prescribing is not necessary, agent still runs no safety checks; if prescribing is necessary, agent may perform contraindication assessment within ASK and/or DIAGNOSE narrative but must still complete CHECK_ALLERGY and CHECK_INTERACTION tool calls before PRESCRIBE.
